title: Easter-Day
content:
I.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How very hard it is to be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A Christian! Hard for you and me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not the mere task of making real
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That duty up to its ideal,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Effecting thus complete and whole,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A purpose or the human soulן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For that is always hard to do;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But hard, I mean, for me and you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To realise it, more or less,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With even the moderate success
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which commonly repays our strife
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To carry out the aims of life.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This aim is greater,ן¿½ you may say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And so more arduous every way.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the importance of the fruits
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still proves to man, in all pursuits,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Proportional encouragement.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then, what if it be Godן¿½s intent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That labour to this one result
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Shall seem unduly difficult?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, thatן¿½s a question in the darkן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the sole thing that I remark
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Upon the difficulty, this;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We do not see it where it is,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the beginning of the race:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As we proceed, it shifts its place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And where we looked for palms to fall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We find the tugן¿½s to come,ן¿½thatן¿½s all.

II.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At first you say, ן¿½The whole, or chief
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of difficulties, is Belief.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Could I believe once thoroughly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The rest were simple. What? Am I
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½An idiot, do you think? A beast?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Prove to me only that the least
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Command of God is Godן¿½s indeed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And what injunction shall I need
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To pay obedience? Death so nigh
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When time must end, eternity
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Begin,ן¿½and cannot I compute?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Weigh loss and gain together? suit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My actions to the balance drawn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And give my body to be sawn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Asunder, hacked in pieces, tied
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To horses, stoned, burned, crucified,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Like any martyr of the list?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How gladly,ן¿½if I made acquist,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Through the brief minutesן¿½ fierce annoy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of Godן¿½s eternity of joy.ן¿½

III.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And certainly you name the point
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereon all turns: for could you joint
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This flexile finite life once tight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into the fixed and infinite,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You, safe inside, would spurn whatן¿½s out,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With carelessness enough, no doubtן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would spurn mere life: but where time brings
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To their next stage your reasonings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your eyes, late wide, begin to wink
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor see the path so well, I think.

IV.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You say, ן¿½Faith may be, one agrees,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A touchstone for Godן¿½s purposes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Even as ourselves conceive of them.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Could He acquit us or condemn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For holding what no hand can loose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Rejecting when we canן¿½t but choose?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As well award the victorן¿½s wreath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To whosoever should take breath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Duly each minute while he livedן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Grant Heaven, because a man contrived
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To see the sunlight every day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He walked forth on the public way.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You must mix some uncertainty
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With faith, if you would have faith be.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why, what but faith, do we abhor
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And idolize each other forן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Faith in our evil, or our good,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Which is or is not understood
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Aright by those we love or those
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We hate, thence called our friends or foes?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Your mistress saw your spiritן¿½s grace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When, turning from the ugly face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I found belief in it too hard;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And both of us have our reward.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet here a doubt peeps: well for us
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Weak beings, to go using thus
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A touchstone for our little ends,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And try with faith the foes and friends;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But God, bethink you! I would fain
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Conceive of the Creatorן¿½s reign
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As based upon exacter laws
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Than creatures build by with applause.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½In all Godן¿½s actsן¿½(as Plato cries
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He doth)ן¿½He should geometrise.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whence, I desiderate . . .

V.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I see!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You would grow smoothly as a tree.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soar heavenward, straightly up like fireן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ God bless youן¿½thereן¿½s your world entire
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Needing no faith, if you think fit;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Go there, walk up and down in it!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The whole creation travails, groansן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Contrive your music from its moans,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Without or let or hindrance, friend!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s an old story, and its end
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As oldן¿½you come back (be sincere)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With every question you put here
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Here where there once was, and is still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We think, a living oracle,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose answers you stood carping at)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This time flung back unanswered flat,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Besides, perhaps, as many more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As those that drove you out before,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now added, where was little need!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Questions impossible, indeed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To us who sate still, all and each
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Persuaded that our earth had speech
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Godן¿½s, writ down, no matter if
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In cursive type or hieroglyph,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Which one fact frees us from the yoke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of guessing why He never spoke.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You come back in no better plight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than when you left us,ן¿½am I right?

VI.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So the old process, I conclude,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Goes on, the reasoningן¿½s pursued
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Further. You own. ן¿½ן¿½Tis well averred,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A scientific faithן¿½s absurd,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Frustrates the very end ן¿½twas meant
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To serve: so I would rest content
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With a mere probability,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But, probable; the chance must lie
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Clear on one side,ן¿½lie all in rough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So long as there is just enough
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To pin my faith to, though it hap
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Only at points: from gap to gap
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½One hangs up a huge curtain so,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Grandly, nor seeks to have it go
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Foldless and flat along the wall:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What care I that some interval
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of life less plainly might depend
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½On God? Iן¿½d hang there to the end;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And thus I should not find it hard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To be a Christian and debarred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From trailing on the earth, till furled
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Away by death!ן¿½Renounce the world?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Were that a mighty hardship? Plan
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A pleasant life, and straight some man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Beside you, with, if he thought fit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Abundant means to compass it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Shall turn deliberate aside
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To try and live as, if you tried
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You clearly might, yet most despise.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½One friend of mine wears out his eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Slighting the stupid joys of sense,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½In patient hope that, ten years hence,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Somewhat completer he may see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½His list of lepidopterן¿½:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½While just the other who most laughs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½At him, above all epitaphs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Aspires to have his tomb describe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Himself as Sole among the tribe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of snuffbox-fanciers, who possessed
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A Grignon with the Regentן¿½s crest.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So that, subduing as you want,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whatever stands predominant
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Among my earthly appetites
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For tastes, and smells, and sounds, and sights,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I shall be doing that alone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To gain a palm-branch and a throne,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Which fifty people undertake
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To do, and gladly, for the sake
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Of giving a Semitic guess,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or playing pawns at blindfold chess.ן¿½

VII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good! and the next thing is,ן¿½look round
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For evidence enough. ן¿½Tis found,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No doubt: as is your sort of mind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So is your sort of searchן¿½youן¿½ll find
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What you desire, and thatן¿½s to be
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A Christian: what says History?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How comforting a point it were
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To find some mummy-scrap declare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There lived a Moses! Better still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Prove Jonahן¿½s whale translatable
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into some quicksand of the seas,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Isle, cavern, rock, or what you please,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Faith might clap her wings and crow
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From such an eminence! Or, noן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Human Heartן¿½s best; you prefer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Making that prove the minister
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To truth; you probe its wants and needs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hopes and fears, then try what creeds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Meet these most aptly,ן¿½resolute
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Faith plucks such substantial fruit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wherever these two correspond,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She little needs to look beyond,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To puzzle out what Orpheus was,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or Dionysius Zagrias.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Youן¿½ll find sufficient, as I say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To satisfy you either way.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You wanted to believe; your pains
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are crownedן¿½you do: and what remains?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Renounce the world!ן¿½Ah, were it done
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By merely cutting one by one
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your limbs off, with your wise head last,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How easy were it!ן¿½how soon past,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If once in the believing mood!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such is manן¿½s usual gratitude,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such thanks to God do we return,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For not exacting that we spurn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A single gift of life, forego
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One real gain,ן¿½only taste them so
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With gravity and temperance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That those mild virtues may enhance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such pleasures, rather than abstractן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Last spice of which, will be the fact
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of love discerned in every gift;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While, when the scene of life shall shift,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the gay heart be taught to ache,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As sorrows and privations take
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The place of joy,ן¿½the thing that seems
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mere misery, under human schemes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Becomes, regarded by the light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Love, as very near, or quite
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As good a gift as joy before.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So plain is it that all the more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Godן¿½s dispensationן¿½s merciful,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More pettishly we try and cull
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Briars, thistles, from our private plot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To mar Godן¿½s ground where thorns are not!

VIII.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Do you say this, or I?ן¿½Oh, you!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then, what, my friend,ן¿½(so I pursue
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our parley)ן¿½you indeed opine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That the Eternal and Divine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did, eighteen centuries ago,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In very truth . . . Enough! you know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The all-stupendous tale,ן¿½that Birth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That Life, that Death! And all, the earth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shuddered at,ן¿½all, the heavens grew black
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rather than see; all, Natureן¿½s rack
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And throe at dissolutionן¿½s brink
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Attested,ן¿½it took place, you think,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Only to give our joys a zest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And prove our sorrows for the best?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We differ, then! Were I, still pale
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And heartstruck at the dreadful tale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Waiting to hear Godן¿½s voice declare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What horror followed for my share,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As implicated in the deed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Apart from other sins,ן¿½concede
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That if He blacked out in a blot
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My brief lifeן¿½s pleasantness, ן¿½twere not
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So very disproportionate!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or there might be another fateן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I certainly could understand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (If fancies were the thing in hand)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How God might save, at that Dayן¿½s price,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The impure in their impurities,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Leave formal licence and complete
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To choose the fair, and pick the sweet.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But there be certain words, broad, plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Uttered again and yet again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hard to mistake, to overglossן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Announcing this worldן¿½s gain for loss,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bidding us reject the same:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The whole world lieth (they proclaim)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In wickedness,ן¿½come out of it!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Turn a deaf ear, if you think fit,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I who thrill through every nerve
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At thought of what deaf ears deserve,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How do you counsel in the case?

IX.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½d take, by all means, in your place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The safe side, since it so appears:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Deny myself, a few brief years,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The natural pleasure, leave the fruit
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or cut the plant up by the root.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Remember what a martyr said
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½On the rude tablet overheadן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½I was born sickly, poor and mean,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½A slave: no misery could screen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½The holders of the pearl of price
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½From Cן¿½sarן¿½s envy; therefore twice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½I fought with beasts, and three times saw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½My children suffer by his lawן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½At last my own release was earned:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½I was some time in being burned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½But at the close a Hand came through
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½The fire above my head, and drew
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½My soul to Christ, whom now I see.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Sergius, a brother, writes for me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½This testimony on the wallן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½For me, I have forgot it all.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You say right; this were not so hard!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And since one nowise is debarred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From this, why not escape some sins
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By such a method?ן¿½

X.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then begins
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the old point, revulsion newן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (For ן¿½tis just this, I bring you to)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If after all we should mistake,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so renounce life for the sake
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of death and nothing else? You hear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our friends we jeered at, send the jeer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Back to ourselves with good effectן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There were my beetles to collect!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My boxן¿½a trifle, I confess,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But here I hold it, neן¿½ertheless!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor idiots, (let us pluck up heart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And answer) we, the better part
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have chosen, though ן¿½twere only hope,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor envy moles like you that grope
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Amid your veritable muck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ More than the grasshoppers would truck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For yours, their passionate life away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That spends itself in leaps all day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To reach the sun, you want the eyes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To see, as they the wings to rise
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And match the noble hearts of them!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, the contemner we contemn,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, when doubt strikes us, so, we ward
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its stroke off, caught upon our guard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not struck enough to overturn
